submission.questionnaireText:
Gautier Marin
Adult Schedule appointment  Edit

1. Initial Health Survey (complete at your earliest convenience)
09/27/2025 at 2:54 PM
Note



1. Do you gain weight easily?
(1) I do not experience this
2. [IF SO] Where do you typically gain weight on your body?
Around the hips
3. Do you feel "puffy," i.e your fat tends to be soft and feel like water retention?
(1) I do not experience this
4. Do you struggle with unintended weight loss?
(1) I do not experience this
5. [WOMEN ONLY] Do you accumulate cellulite?
Not Applicable
6. Do you struggle with low appetite?
(3) This is a fairly bothersome issue for me
7. Do you tend to wake up when sleeping?
(5) This is an extremely bothersome issue for me
8. If you tend to wake up when sleeping, is it: within the first few hours of falling asleep OR later on in the night OR both?
Both
9. If you tend to wake up when sleeping, is it to: urinate OR eat something OR drink something OR seemingly no reason?
Urinate
10. If you tend to wake up when sleeping, do you find that you are hot or sweaty upon waking?
Yes
11. Do you NOT dream?
(1) I do not experience this
12. Do you experience nightmares or sleep paralysis?
(2) This is a somewhat bothersome issue for me
13. Do you have trouble falling asleep?
(2) This is a somewhat bothersome issue for me
14. Do you feel like you DON'T get a restful sleep?
(5) This is an extremely bothersome issue for me
15. Do you frequently feel cold, especially at the extremities (fingertips, toes, and nose)?
(4) This is a very bothersome issue for me
16. [WOMEN ONLY] Do you frequently have painful menstrual cramps?
Not Applicable
17. [WOMEN ONLY] Do you frequently have irregular menstruation or suffer from premenstrual syndrome?
Not Applicable
18. Do you suffer from chronic congestion without mucus?
(1) I do not experience this
19. Do you frequently experience mucus buildup in the throat or nose?
(1) I do not experience this
20. [IF SO] Is it exacerbated after eating?
Not Applicable
21. Do you suffer from dental problems such as bad breath, cavities, and gum inflammation?
(3) This is a fairly bothersome issue for me
22. Do you often find that you are irritable or easily agitated?
(1) I do not experience this
23. Do you suffer from "brain fog," low energy or feel like you do not have mental clarity?
(2) This is a somewhat bothersome issue for me
24. Do you tend to have an "afternoon crash" after eating lunch?
(1) I do not experience this
25. Do you often feel "hyper aware," jittery, or restless?
(1) I do not experience this
26. Do you notice the sensation of your heart pounding or difficulty breathing?
(1) I do not experience this
27. Have you had a loss of pleasure in activities you normally enjoy?
(1) I do not experience this
28. Have you noticed a lack of sexual interest or desire?
(1) I do not experience this
29. [MEN ONLY] Have you lost morning erections?
(2) This is a somewhat bothersome issue for me
30. Do you have skin issues such as dry skin, eczema, or acne?
(1) I do not experience this
31. Can you trace a worsening of your symptoms back to a traumatic event?
No
32. Do you have specific food cravings? If so, please list the foods you crave.
No
33. Do you have a history of mental health issues? If so, please list them.
No
34. Do you experience constipation?
(4) This is a very bothersome issue for me
35. Do you experience nausea?
(1) I do not experience this
36. Do you experience bloating (subjective feeling)?
(5) This is an extremely bothersome issue for me
37. Do you experience distention (visible expansion of the abdomen)?
(5) This is an extremely bothersome issue for me
38. Do you experience diarrhea?
(1) I do not experience this
39. Do you experience belching?
(5) This is an extremely bothersome issue for me
40. Do you experience flatulence?
(5) This is an extremely bothersome issue for me
41. Do you experience pain in your upper abdomen?
(2) This is a somewhat bothersome issue for me
42. Do you experience pain in your right upper quadrant?
(1) I do not experience this
43. Do you experience pain in your lower abdomen?
(5) This is an extremely bothersome issue for me
44. Are you aware of any specific foods that trigger your symptoms? If so, please list them.
Yes
Fodmaps, and more specifically onions, garlic, whole grains and cruciferous vegetables
45. Do you experience dry stools?
(5) This is an extremely bothersome issue for me
46. Do you experience acid reflux?
(2) This is a somewhat bothersome issue for me
47. Do your symptoms worsen IMMEDIATELY after eating?
(1) I do not experience this
48. Do your symptoms worsen 1-3 hours after eating?
(4) This is a very bothersome issue for me
49. Do symptoms alleviate after a bowel movement?
Sometimes
50. Have you tried anything in the past to alleviate your symptoms? These can be dietary changes, supplements, prescriptions or anything else. If so, please list them, and how helpful (or unhelpful) they were for you. Please be as specific as possible, including specific foods and brands and dosages of supplements, as well as what symptoms they affected.
Yes
I've tried many things, some of which I don't precisely remember, but here is what I can gather.
Diets:
Low fodmap: Helps with bloating a bit, but incredibly restrictive and I'm still super bloated and gassy
Carnivore: Helped with bloating, but was still bloated and increased pain
Vegetarian: No help
No gluten: Decreased bloating a little bit
No lactose: Decreased bloating a little bit
Medication (none helped, I don't have the dosage for all):
Clamoxyl (Amoxicilline): 2g/day for 7 days in 2018. It was after I had my wisdom teeth removed. Worsened symptoms.
Solupred (Prednisolone): 70mg/day for 7 days in 2017
Laroxyl: 25mg/day in 2018
Tolexine Gé
Pentasa: 4g/day in 2018
Tinidazole for giardia in 2019
Clamoxyl (Amoxicilline) again for strep throat in 2020
Supplements:
Various probiotics: don't remember them all, but I do remember being given Megasporebiotics and Restorflora specifically. Didn't help.
D3K2 Thorne
Multivitamins, B complex (thorne)
Atrantil in 2018
Sunfiber prebiotic in 2024 (made me more bloated)
SPM Supreme in 2024
California Gold Prodigest
Psyllium husk
Mega IGG
Eventually I tend to stop all supplements because they either don't help or worsen things, except d3k2.
Things I haven't tried but considered:
B1 megadosing
51. Are you currently taking any supplements? If so, please list them and be as specific as possible, including the brand and dosage of any supplement. Please mention how helpful (or unhelpful) you perceive them to be.
Yes
Vitamin D3K2 thorne , 2k IU / day
52. Are you currently taking any medications? If so, please list them and be as specific as possible, including the brand and dosage of any medication.
No
53. Are you currently diagnosed with any medical conditions? If so, please list them.
Yes
Post infectious IBS, Dysbiosis
Scoliosis, L5S1 degenerative disc disease
Piles
Migraines
Labial herpes
54. Please describe your diet. What foods do you typically eat, and in what amounts? Are these foods prepared by you, or are they pre-packaged, or from a restaurant? At what times do you typically eat?
Typical day of eating:

Morning: 150g of fat-free yoghurt, 1 spoon of raw organic honey, 1 fruit (banana, kiwi, …)

Lunch: 150-200g of lean protein (chicken breast, salmon, sometimes eggs), white rice and vegetables in relatively small quantity (usually zucchinis, cucumbers, carrots or tomatoes) + 1 tablespoon olive oil + 10g of parmesan cheese

Snack: Fat free yogurt or milk + 30g of whey protein + some berries + a spoon of honey

Dinner: Protein (same as lunch, twice a week lean red meat like 5% ground beef or fillet), carbs (buckwheat pasta, millet, …) + 1 tablespoon olive oil + 10g of parmesan

Everything is organic. Once a week I'll eat a macro-friendly "cheat meal" like a homemade pizza. I will occasionally eat out, maybe 2-3 times a month. I don't drink alcohol.

55. Please describe your lifestyle. Do you get outside often? Do you exercise? How stressful is your day-to-day life due to work or other factors?
I work from home, so pretty sedentary. I try to walk 10k steps a day. I recently started working out 3 times a week, but I find it hard to be consistent because of bloating and lack of sleep.

My day to day is not stressful at all, the only thing that makes me anxious is my health.

56. What are your health primary goals you are looking achieve?
Above all, fix my digestion. Being bloated and having so much gas and pain everyday is killing my quality of life. I can't sleep because of it.

After that, I would like to address my other issues like high cholesterol, moderate fatty liver, poor sleep, low testosterone, but I feel they might all get better if I fix my gut first.

57. Have you ever had food poisoning before? If so, when?
Yes, many times, but the one big time was in 2017, when I traveled to Mexico and got infected with giardia.

58. Please list your height, weight and waist measurements.
Height 184cm, weight 76kg, waist 88cm

59. Please list any other information you think would help us help you.
Here's a quick recap of my health journey.

I started getting gut pain as a child. I would get this weird dull pain in my lower abdomen during class maybe once or twice a month, and the only way to relieve it would be to go to the nursery or home and lie down. Doctors did some tests but didn't find anything.

Things started getting worse around 18, I was in an intensive school program studying 80 hours a week, not getting much sleep and not eating super well. These "gut pain" episodes got more frequent and I started getting more bloated.

Then a few years later, during college, again not having the best diet and drinking quite a bit, I developed daily bloating / distension. But things really got bad after a trip to Mexico in 2017 where I got food poisoning and later was diagnosed with giardia. From this point on, I was in pain 24/7 and extremely bloated / gassy all the time. I especially got pain in the lower right quadrant. That's when I started seeing many doctors and doing all kinds of tests.

In 2020, I got surgery for appendicitis. I believe I was suffering from an underdiagnosed condition called chronic appendicitis prior to that. After the surgery, my lower quadrant pain got away in a few months (after two and half years of suffering), but I still had pain around the belly button during flare ups. I was still and still am to this day extremely distended and gassy.

Because of that, I tend to wake up 3-5 times every night, and often stay awake for more than an hour. Mostly because of trapped gas pain.

I also have chronically high cortisol, probably because I haven't gotten proper sleep and pain-free days for the past 7 years.

One interesting thing is that my sister also suffers from distention / severe bloating (although it does not impact her life as much), so there might be a genetic component at play.

Sometimes my pain "flares up" and I can feel inflammation around my belly button. I believe the part that gets inflamed is my terminal ileum. They found lymphoid hyperplasia there when they did colonoscopy. Also every time I've done imaging, my colon was always completely full of gas and matter. In the morning, I can feel that some gas is still "stuck" in my terminal intestine. Interestingly, things tend to get better when I eat, probably because then gas gets "pushed down" past the ileum and I get a few hours of peace, until they accumulate again (pain tends to come 5-6 hours after meal). Dismotility is most likely a causal factor in all of this.

Apart from that, I have always had a "weaker" physical build than most people. I'm skinny fat, and I have a hard time gaining muscle. My immune system is also quite weak, whenever I have flare ups and can't sleep, I tend to get sick right after. When I have a night out, or when I wake up earlier than usual, there's a good chance I'll get sick because of lack of sleep, and I will almost certainly get a headache.

One thing to note is that whenever my gut issues "improve" (for reasons I haven't been able to identify, and it's never to the point that my QoL is good), then the headaches and other symptoms go away too. That's why I believe that fixing my gut would help with every other issue that I have.

Before wrapping up, I want to quickly address my high cholesterol and moderate fatty liver. I wasn't suffering from this before 2017. In 2017, my ldl-c was around 1.3 g/L (and I was already very bloated). Then over the years it rose to the current level of 2.04 g/L. I've asked around and nobody in my family has high cholesterol or died of heart attacks. It doesn't mean it's not genetic, but I suspect there is more to it than that. Also I don't drink and I'm not following a high fat diet so docs don't understand why I got the fatty liver. I may be wrong but I believe it's linked to my gut issues. One thing I'm considering is that since things started to get bad in 2017, I've had a hard time with fiber. Also I've been following a low fodmap diet for years. So perhaps the rise in cholesterol is related to the absence of fiber somehow, but it's hard for me to test that theory because I'm already extremely bloated without the fiber.

Finally, here are a few interesting tidbits that may be helpful (or not):

No fever since childhood. Even during strep throat or appendicitis. Don't know why. Doctors don't seem to care.

Labial herpes whenever I'm too tired.

Bleeding gums when I brush (I use extra soft brush)
submission.takehomeText:
Gautier Marin
Adult Schedule appointment  Edit

3. Take Home Assessment Inputs (reference PDF)
10/10/2025 at 8:08 AM
Note



What are your four resting heart beats per minute? Please separate values with commas. [example: 78, 81, 75, 80]
64, 65, 66, 64

How many times did you urinate each day for four days? Please separate values with commas. [example: 6, 6, 8, 7]
5, 6, 7, 6

On average, how foamy was your urine?
(2) Somewhat foamy
How many times did you defecate per day for four days? Please separate values with commas. [example: 3, 1, 0, 2]
1, 1, 0, 1

What Types on the Bristol Stool Scale did each of your bowel movements fall under? Please separate values with commas. [example: Type 2, Type 2, Type 4, Type 5, Type 7, Type 1, Type 6]
Please describe any other abnormalities of the stool observed, such as altered color or undigested food in the stool.
1, 2, 1

What were both of your temperature measurements each morning? Please separate values with commas. [example:
98.2, 98.4]

96.6, 97.2, 97.2, 97.3

[WOMEN ONLY] What date did your last period begin? [MEN: SKIP QUESTION]
No answer given.

[WOMEN ONLY] What date did you take your first temperature measurement? [MEN: SKIP QUESTION]
No answer given.
submission.advisorNotesText:
Appointment #1
With: Collin Dees 
Billing code: 
consulting
9:00 AM – 10:00 AM
Progress Note 
Gold Consult 1/3

The client's primary goals were to improve gut health, resolve persistent bloating and distension, address elevated cholesterol levels, and manage the interconnected effects these issues have on sleep, stress, and overall health. These digestive concerns have significantly impacted quality of life and are affecting sleep patterns, which in turn impacts stress levels, cholesterol, body fat, and immune system function.

Gastrointestinal symptoms began in childhood with lower abdomen pain that progressively worsened over time. By age 18, these symptoms had significantly impacted daily life. Multiple gastroenterologists were consulted, but their treatments proved ineffective. A gut infection contracted in Mexico further exacerbated the existing condition. Appendicitis surgery provided substantial improvement in quality of life, suggesting the presence of chronic appendicitis. Following the surgery, pain in the lower right quadrant ceased completely, though motility and microbiome issues have persisted. Currently, food consistently triggers bloating, gas, and pain, creating a challenging cycle of symptoms.

The daily symptom pattern follows a predictable course. Mornings bring the best relief, with symptoms manageable after lunch, but by late afternoon, significant bloating and distension develop. This bloating severely impacts appetite, making dinner consumption difficult. Sleep is disrupted by trapped gas that causes painful awakening around 6 a.m., preventing return to sleep. Gas passage occurs in the morning, and the cycle repeats. There's a sensation of something becoming stuck around the connection between the small and large intestine, requiring movement to facilitate gas passage, suggesting abnormal motility patterns.

Dietary management has become extremely restrictive due to multiple food triggers. FODMAP foods including fructose, gluten, spices, and fibrous foods consistently trigger symptoms. Tolerated foods are limited to white rice, lean meats, and specific vegetables like carrots, zucchini, and cucumbers. Foods that are "impossible to digest" include pasta, fatty meats, cruciferous vegetables, and fibrous fruits. Fiber supplements like psyllium husk and FODMAP-friendly options such as PHGG and Sunfiber cause extreme bloating. Recently introduced oats in 30-gram portions are tolerated and provide some cholesterol benefit. Even small amounts of fibrous foods, such as 50 grams of blueberries as a snack, can eliminate appetite for subsequent meals due to the excessive bloating and lack of progression through the GI tract. Gluten specifically triggers migraines, though buckwheat, consumed infrequently, is tolerated without causing migraines. Fructose malabsorption has developed over time, as excessive fructose intake now increases gas production, which wasn't the case during childhood. Coffee causes immediate diarrhea and gut irritation, while garlic triggers increased gas and pain.

Constipation remains a persistent issue despite attempts to optimize circadian rhythm through morning sunlight exposure and following established recommendations. This constipation creates fermentation and gas production, perpetuating the vicious cycle of bloating and distension. Daily caloric intake is approximately 1800 calories due to reduced appetite from symptoms.l

Previous treatment attempts have been extensive but largely unsuccessful. Multiple courses of antibiotics including amoxicillin and doxycycline provided no benefit and worsened symptoms. Herbal antimicrobials such as berberine and oregano oil caused significant pain. A SIBO tincture containing concentrated oregano and berberine from a previous practitioner had to be discontinued after three days due to severe pain. Digestive enzymes haven't been attempted in the past three to four years. Various probiotics have been tried across different strains but generally with poor response, though S. boulardii hasn't been tested. IgG supplementation proved ineffective. Collagen supplementation in the past was also ineffective.

Supplements that haven't been tried include lactoferrin, l-glutamine, sodium butyrate, and zinc-carnosine. The approach to new supplements is cautious, preferring gradual introduction one at a time to assess tolerance, as adding multiple supplements simultaneously makes it difficult to identify problematic ones.

Cholesterol levels have risen progressively over the years, potentially related to dietary factors, lack of exercise, gut dysfunction, or liver issues. Recent blood work from September 17th revealed elevated liver enzymes, likely attributed to recently initiated exercise routines. Moderate fatty liver disease was confirmed by ultrasound approximately one year ago.

Cortisol levels have been chronically elevated, with regular testing conducted over the past seven years. Cushing's disease has been ruled out through appropriate testing. High cortisol contributes to a cycle involving bad nightmares, bloating, and pain, creating ongoing anxiety. Two months ago, blood work confirmed high cortisol levels, prompting dietary and lifestyle modifications that have led to some improvements.

Thyroid function concerns include consistently low body temperatures ranging from 96.6 to 97.2 Fahrenheit. TSH and free T4 have been checked, but free T3 has not been evaluated. Thyroid hormone supplementation has never been attempted.

Testosterone levels were found to be low in blood work from May of the previous year, along with low vitamin D levels at that time.

Current supplementation includes a multivitamin with methylfolate and B vitamins, vitamin D at 4000 IU daily (previously 2000 IU), and magnesium bisglycinate at night. Vitamin D levels have improved to the upper range following supplementation. B12 and folate levels were previously low, making current supplementation beneficial. Selenium levels were found to be low. Homocysteine was elevated, indicating potential deficiencies in B6, B9, B12, or glycine.

Family history reveals similar but less severe symptoms in a sister who is two years younger (client is 31 years old). The sister experiences gas and bloating but with significantly less pain intensity.

Lifestyle factors include regular exercise aimed at reducing body fat to improve liver function and cholesterol levels. A clean diet is maintained with careful tracking of all food intake. Gluten is avoided due to genetic markers for celiac disease. No smoking or nicotine consumption occurs. Significant mold exposure history is not suspected, and antifungal medications have not been used.

Previous testing has included stool tests showing low secretory IgA and some Klebsiella presence, though SIBO breath tests were negative. A GI 360 test confirmed low secretory IgA. Genova intestinal permeability testing showed normal mannitol-lactulose ratio. Organic acids testing led to various supplement recommendations. Recent SIBO testing revealed malabsorption with elevated hydrogen levels, suggesting fructose malabsorption. A vibrant micronutrient panel identified genetic markers for celiac disease.

5

Waking up when sleeping

Not getting restful sleep

Bloating

Distention

Belching

Flatulence

Pain in lower abdomen

Dry stools

4

Constipation

Symptoms worsening 1-3 hours after eating

3

Fodmaps, and more specifically onions, garlic, whole grains and cruciferous vegetables

Mental health issues

None

Tried

I've tried many things, some of which I don't precisely remember, but here is what I can gather.
Diets:

Low fodmap: Helps with bloating a bit, but incredibly restrictive and I'm still super bloated and gassy

Carnivore: Helped with bloating, but was still bloated and increased pain

Vegetarian: No help

No gluten: Decreased bloating a little bit

No lactose: Decreased bloating a little bit
Medication (none helped, I don't have the dosage for all):

Clamoxyl (Amoxicilline): 2g/day for 7 days in 2018. It was after I had my wisdom teeth removed. Worsened symptoms.

Solupred (Prednisolone): 70mg/day for 7 days in 2017

Supplements:

Diet/Lifestyle

Typical day of eating:

Morning: 150g of fat-free yoghurt, 1 spoon of raw organic honey, 1 fruit (banana, kiwi, …)

Lunch: 150-200g of lean protein (chicken breast, salmon, sometimes eggs), white rice and vegetables in relatively small quantity (usually zucchinis, cucumbers, carrots or tomatoes) + 1 tablespoon olive oil + 10g of parmesan cheese

Snack: Fat free yogurt or milk + 30g of whey protein + some berries + a spoon of honey

Dinner: Protein (same as lunch, twice a week lean red meat like 5% ground beef or fillet), carbs (buckwheat pasta, millet, …) + 1 tablespoon olive oil + 10g of parmesan

Everything is organic. Once a week I'll eat a macro-friendly "cheat meal" like a homemade pizza. I will occasionally eat out, maybe 2-3 times a month. I don't drink alcohol.

I work from home, so pretty sedentary. I try to walk 10k steps a day. I recently started working out 3 times a week, but I find it hard to be consistent because of bloating and lack of sleep.

My day to day is not stressful at all, the only thing that makes me anxious is my health.

Goals

Above all, fix my digestion. Being bloated and having so much gas and pain everyday is killing my quality of life. I can't sleep because of it.

After that, I would like to address my other issues like high cholesterol, moderate fatty liver, poor sleep, low testosterone, but I feel they might all get better if I fix my gut first.

Other information

Here's a quick recap of my health journey.

I started getting gut pain as a child. I would get this weird dull pain in my lower abdomen during class maybe once or twice a month, and the only way to relieve it would be to go to the nursery or home and lie down. Doctors did some tests but didn't find anything.

Things started getting worse around 18, I was in an intensive school program studying 80 hours a week, not getting much sleep and not eating super well. These "gut pain" episodes got more frequent and I started getting more bloated.

Then a few years later, during college, again not having the best diet and drinking quite a bit, I developed daily bloating / distension. But things really got bad after a trip to Mexico in 2017 where I got food poisoning and later was diagnosed with giardia. From this point on, I was in pain 24/7 and extremely bloated / gassy all the time. I especially got pain in the lower right quadrant. That's when I started seeing many doctors and doing all kinds of tests.

In 2020, I got surgery for appendicitis. I believe I was suffering from an underdiagnosed condition called chronic appendicitis prior to that. After the surgery, my lower quadrant pain got away in a few months (after two and half years of suffering), but I still had pain around the belly button during flare ups. I was still and still am to this day extremely distended and gassy.

Because of that, I tend to wake up 3-5 times every night, and often stay awake for more than an hour. Mostly because of trapped gas pain.

I also have chronically high cortisol, probably because I haven't gotten proper sleep and pain-free days for the past 7 years.

One interesting thing is that my sister also suffers from distention / severe bloating (although it does not impact her life as much), so there might be a genetic component at play.

Sometimes my pain "flares up" and I can feel inflammation around my belly button. I believe the part that gets inflamed is my terminal ileum. They found lymphoid hyperplasia there when they did colonoscopy. Also every time I've done imaging, my colon was always completely full of gas and matter. In the morning, I can feel that some gas is still "stuck" in my terminal intestine. Interestingly, things tend to get better when I eat, probably because then gas gets "pushed down" past the ileum and I get a few hours of peace, until they accumulate again (pain tends to come 5-6 hours after meal). Dismotility is most likely a causal factor in all of this.

Apart from that, I have always had a "weaker" physical build than most people. I'm skinny fat, and I have a hard time gaining muscle. My immune system is also quite weak, whenever I have flare ups and can't sleep, I tend to get sick right after. When I have a night out, or when I wake up earlier than usual, there's a good chance I'll get sick because of lack of sleep, and I will almost certainly get a headache.

One thing to note is that whenever my gut issues "improve" (for reasons I haven't been able to identify, and it's never to the point that my QoL is good), then the headaches and other symptoms go away too. That's why I believe that fixing my gut would help with every other issue that I have.

Before wrapping up, I want to quickly address my high cholesterol and moderate fatty liver. I wasn't suffering from this before 2017. In 2017, my ldl-c was around 1.3 g/L (and I was already very bloated). Then over the years it rose to the current level of 2.04 g/L. I've asked around and nobody in my family has high cholesterol or died of heart attacks. It doesn't mean it's not genetic, but I suspect there is more to it than that. Also I don't drink and I'm not following a high fat diet so docs don't understand why I got the fatty liver. I may be wrong but I believe it's linked to my gut issues. One thing I'm considering is that since things started to get bad in 2017, I've had a hard time with fiber. Also I've been following a low fodmap diet for years. So perhaps the rise in cholesterol is related to the absence of fiber somehow, but it's hard for me to test that theory because I'm already extremely bloated without the fiber.

Finally, here are a few interesting tidbits that may be helpful (or not):

No fever since childhood. Even during strep throat or appendicitis. Don't know why. Doctors don't seem to care.

Labial herpes whenever I'm too tired.

Bleeding gums when I brush (I use extra soft brush)

Height, weight and waits measurements - 184cm - 6’0”, 76kg - 167.5lb, 88cm - 34.6 inch

Temps - 96.6, 97.2, 97.2, 97.3

White tongue - 2/4?

Notable Lab Findings

- Date: September 17th (recent blood work)

Elevated liver enzymes

B12: low

Folate: low

- Date: Two months ago

Cortisol: high
- Date: May of last year

Free testosterone: low

Vitamin D: low (now in upper range with supplementation)

- Previous results:

TSH and free T4: checked (specific values not provided)

Homocysteine: high

Selenium levels: low

- Stool testing (last year, U.S.):

Secretory IgA: low

Klebsiella: present

SIBO breath tests: negative

- GI 360 test:

Secretory IgA: low
- Genova intestinal permeability test:

Mannitol-lactulose ratio: normal
- Organic acids testing (Mosaic Diagnostics):

4-hydroxyhippuric acid: low

3-indoleacetic acid: low

Beta-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate: detected

- Vibrant micronutrient panel:

Gene for celiac disease: positive
- SIBO test:

Hydrogen levels: elevated (indicating fructose malabsorption)
- Ultrasound (one year ago):

Moderate fatty liver confirmed
A/P

31-year-old male with chronic gastrointestinal symptoms including bloating, distension, and abdominal pain since childhood, with associated sleep disruption, elevated cholesterol, and fatty liver disease.

Chronic gastrointestinal symptoms with motility dysfunction

Assessment: Longstanding gastrointestinal symptoms beginning in childhood and significantly worsening at age 18. Previous appendectomy resolved lower right quadrant pain but persistent motility and microbiome issues remain. Current symptoms include daily bloating and distension that worsen throughout the day, trapped gas causing nocturnal awakening around 6 AM, constipation, and food intolerances. SIBO breath test shows malabsorption with elevated hydrogen levels suggesting fructose malabsorption. Previous stool testing revealed low secretory IgA and Klebsiella presence. Organic acids testing shows 4-hydroxyhippuric acid deficiency indicating gut microbiome imbalances and low polyphenols, and low 3-indoleacetic acid suggesting gastrointestinal antibiotic effects. Positive genetic markers for celiac disease are present and experiences migraines with gluten exposure. Symptoms create a cycle with sleep disruption and anxiety. Client tolerates limited foods including white rice, lean meats, carrots, zucchini, and cucumbers, but cannot tolerate fibrous foods, cruciferous vegetables, or FODMAP supplements. Previous antibiotic courses and herbal antimicrobials were ineffective or caused worsening symptoms.

Plan:

- Glycine supplementation up to 3 grams before bed to help with sleep and gut health. Need indicated by high homocysteine and low glycine on testing. He thought taking magnesium glycinate was sufficient. I advised him otherwise.

- Start masszymes or whatever other good blend is easiest for him to order. He has not tried them in 3-4 years.

- Start L-glutamine 10g daily in liquid sipped throughout the day week 1. If no issues, add in lactoferrin on empty stomach 250-350mg daily week 2. If no issues, add zinc carnosine 75mg once daily week 3. If no issues, add in tributyrin 300mg once daily week 4. Then on week 5, add S. boulardii and Clostridium Butyricum/Akkermansia https://pendulumlife.com/pages/shop-pendulum-metabolic-daily-probiotic-energy-metabolism

- Has issues with consuming garlic, but if all is going well above I would like to add in Metagenics SuperGarlic 6000(the strongest OTC allicin I have been able to find) 6000mcg(6mg) in one tablet. One clove of garlic has around the same amount of allicin. He can start by adding in 1/2 tablet for one week once daily, then if well tolerated increase to 1/2 tablet twice daily for another week. After 2 weeks on, take 2 weeks off and start cycle again. Max dosage 1/2 tablet three times daily.

- Ginger for motility. I think capsules would be fine, but if he can do a tea at night with the glycine powder, that could be good as well.

- 7mg Nicotine patch transdermal over the area of the ileocecal valve. Can be cut in half and try that first. Acetylcholinergic for motility plus activation of vagus nerve.

- Continue current dietary restrictions avoiding FODMAP foods, gluten, spices, and fibrous foods. Follow our standard eating plan in the Prism report.

- Continue current supplements: multivitamin with methylfolate and B vitamins, magnesium bisglycinate at night, vitamin D 4000 IU daily

Elevated liver enzymes with fatty liver

Assessment: Recent blood work from September 17th showed elevated liver enzymes, likely related to recent initiation of exercise program. Gautier has moderate fatty liver disease confirmed by ultrasound one year ago. Elevated enzymes may be related to fatty liver disease rather than alcohol or medication use.

Plan:

- GGT testing as better confirmatory test for liver function

- Continue exercise program to increase muscle mass and reduce weight

- Continue dietary modifications to address fatty liver

Possible thyroid dysfunction

Assessment: Reports low body temperatures ranging 96.6-97.2°F. Previous testing included TSH and free T4 but not free T3. Given chronic high cortisol levels, client likely has high reverse T3 and low free T3. Chronic high cortisol has been present for 7 years with Cushing's disease ruled out.

Plan:

- Consider further thyroid testing to confirm reverse T3 and free T3 levels

- Consider low-dose T3 supplementation, he was very hesitant about this and wants to wait for testing

- Add L-tyrosine to report as precursor to thyroid hormone and dopamine

- Continue vitamin D supplementation at current dose

Nutritional deficiencies

Assessment: Previous testing showed low B12 and folate levels, high homocysteine indicating potential deficiencies in B6, B9, B12, or glycine, and low selenium levels. Vitamin D levels now in upper range after supplementation.

Plan:

- Continue current multivitamin with methylfolate and B vitamins for B12 and folate deficiency

- Continue vitamin D 4000 IU daily

Things I will consider adding in future: Pomegranate extract or urolithin a, low dose naltrexone

ok he uploaded a ton of tests, the only one you have to go through is the standard labs from this year

include these on report

Klebsiella pneumoniae – 2nd quartile (ref: undetectable)
Klebsiella pneumoniae is an opportunistic gram-negative bacterium that can inhabit the gut. When present above undetectable levels, it may suggest dysbiosis or microbial imbalance. Overgrowth of this organism can promote inflammation, intestinal permeability, and the release of lipopolysaccharides (endotoxins).

Secretory IgA (sIgA) – 1.4 mg/dL (ref: 30–275)
explanation in model

Butyrate – 1 mg/mL (ref: >2 mg/mL)
Butyrate is a short-chain fatty acid produced by beneficial bacteria fermenting dietary fibers. Low levels indicate a lack of fiber fermentation or a deficit in butyrate-producing bacteria such as Faecalibacterium prausnitzii. This can contribute to reduced energy for colonocytes, inflammation, and impaired gut barrier integrity.

RC-II+III activity – 13% (ref: >60%)
Respiratory chain complexes II and III are components of the mitochondrial electron transport chain responsible for ATP generation. Low activity suggests mitochondrial dysfunction or oxidative stress impairing energy metabolism. This can manifest as fatigue, metabolic inefficiency, or impaired detoxification capacity.

Citrate synthase activity – 194% (ref: 60–140%)
Citrate synthase is a mitochondrial enzyme used as a marker of mitochondrial mass or biogenesis. Elevated activity often indicates a compensatory increase in mitochondrial number due to inefficient energy production. It may reflect a response to oxidative stress or mitochondrial damage where more mitochondria are produced to maintain ATP output.

Serine – 5.8 μmol/dL (ref: 2–5)
Serine is a nonessential amino acid involved in protein synthesis, phospholipid formation, and neurotransmitter support. Slightly elevated levels may indicate increased protein catabolism or impaired conversion into glycine. It can also suggest metabolic stress in one-carbon metabolism pathways. High serine and low glycine indicate

Glycine – 7 μmol/dL (ref: >10)
Glycine is an inhibitory neurotransmitter and key cofactor for glutathione synthesis and methylation balance. Low levels suggest poor antioxidant capacity or insufficient methylation substrate recycling. It may contribute to impaired detoxification and increased oxidative stress.

Ethanolamine – 1.01 μmol/dL (ref:
0.31–0.74)
Ethanolamine is a precursor for phosphatidylethanolamine, a major phospholipid in cell membranes. Elevated levels suggest breakdown or turnover of membrane phospholipids, possibly due to oxidative or inflammatory stress. It can also indicate altered lipid metabolism or mitochondrial membrane instability.

β-Alanine – 0.7 μmol/dL (ref: <0.6)
β-Alanine is involved in carnosine synthesis, which buffers acid in muscles and protects against oxidative stress. Mild elevation may reflect increased amino acid turnover or impaired utilization into carnosine. It can occur in states of oxidative stress, B6 deficiency, or altered muscle metabolism.

Homocysteine – 14.1 μmol/L (in model)
Homocysteine is an intermediate in methionine metabolism requiring folate, B12, and B6 for clearance. Elevated levels indicate impaired methylation or deficiencies in these vitamins. Chronic elevation is associated with endothelial dysfunction, cardiovascular risk, and oxidative stress.

Selenium – 92 mcg/L (ref: 109–330)
Selenium is an essential trace mineral required for glutathione peroxidase and thyroid hormone metabolism. Low levels suggest inadequate intake or poor absorption and can reduce antioxidant defense. Deficiency may impair thyroid function, immune response, and detoxification capacity.

Gamma-tocopherol – 0.6 mg/L (ref:
0.7–4.9)
Gamma-tocopherol is one form of vitamin E with strong anti-inflammatory and nitrogen-reactive scavenging activity. Low levels suggest reduced antioxidant protection or high oxidative stress consuming vitamin E stores. It can also reflect poor dietary intake or lipid absorption issues.

Glutathione – 571 μmol/L (ref: >669)
Glutathione is the body’s master antioxidant responsible for detoxification and redox balance. Low levels indicate oxidative stress, toxin burden, or insufficient precursors such as cysteine, glycine, or glutamate. This reduction can impair mitochondrial function and increase cellular vulnerability to damage.

Formiminoglutamate (FIGLU) – 2.3 mmol/mol creatinine (ref: <1.5)
FIGLU is an intermediate in histidine metabolism that accumulates when folate is insufficient. Elevated levels indicate folate deficiency or impaired folate-dependent one-carbon metabolism. This can disrupt DNA synthesis, repair, and methylation processes.

Xanthurenate – 1.01 mmol/mol creatinine (ref: <0.96)
Xanthurenate is a metabolite in the tryptophan pathway that increases when vitamin B6 (pyridoxal-5-phosphate) is low. Elevated levels indicate functional B6 deficiency and impaired tryptophan metabolism. This imbalance can contribute to mood issues, poor neurotransmitter synthesis, and oxidative stress.

Coenzyme Q10 – 31.5 pg/MM WBC (ref:
39.6–225.3)
Coenzyme Q10 (ubiquinone) is a vital component of the mitochondrial electron transport chain and functions as a lipid-soluble antioxidant. Low intracellular levels suggest impaired mitochondrial energy production and increased oxidative stress. This may occur due to statin use, nutrient deficiencies (especially B vitamins), or chronic illness that elevates oxidative demand.

Cysteine – 50.1 pg/MM WBC (ref:
60.0–565.0)
Cysteine is a sulfur-containing amino acid essential for synthesizing glutathione, the body’s main antioxidant. Low levels indicate reduced capacity for detoxification and antioxidant regeneration. This deficiency can result from oxidative stress, insufficient dietary sulfur amino acids, or impaired conversion from methionine due to B6 or B12 deficiency.

red light exposure - Red light therapy enhances mitochondrial respiration by stimulating cytochrome c oxidase, the terminal enzyme in complex IV of the electron transport chain. This increases ATP output and reduces oxidative stress, indirectly supporting complex II and III function by improving overall electron flow efficiency. It can also upregulate mitochondrial biogenesis and antioxidant enzymes, helping restore balance when electron transport activity is impaired.

coQ10- Coenzyme Q10 acts as the electron carrier between complexes I/II and III, making it critical for proper energy transfer within the mitochondrial chain. When complex II/III activity is low, CoQ10 supplementation can improve electron transport efficiency and reduce reactive oxygen species buildup. Adequate CoQ10 levels also stabilize mitochondrial membranes and enhance overall cellular energy metabolism.
